data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a synchronous systems failure of palatial redistributive economies due to a common vulnerability: over-specialized, top-heavy administrative structures that could not adapt to a prolonged multi-year drought, leading to a cascading loss of legitimacy, elite infighting, and eventual abandonment of the palace centers.", "mechanism": "Prolonged drought (attested in paleoclimate proxies) reduced agricultural yields below the threshold required to sustain the non-producing administrative and craft-specialist classes within the palace system. As granaries emptied, the redistributive 'social contract' failed. Palaces could no longer pay rations to dependent workers or provide famine relief, triggering internal revolts, elite fragmentation, and a shift to smaller, more resilient subsistence strategies. The high interconnectivity of these economies (through gift exchange, mercenaries, and trade) meant the failure of one node (e.g., Mycenae) disrupted luxury trade and diplomatic marriages, accelerating the legitimacy crisis in others (e.g., Hittites, Ugarit). Egypt survived because the Nile's floodplain agriculture was less susceptible to the same drought pattern and its more centralized, divine-king ideology could absorb the shock without total administrative collapse.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria", "Cyprus (initially)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows palace storerooms were full of grain and oil at the time of destruction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Paleoclimate data reveals a wet, high-yield period precisely during 1200-1150 BC in the Eastern Mediterranean.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B tablets from Pylos show no evidence of military preparations or rationing in the year before destruction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Builds directly on the 'common vulnerability' kernel from artifact 278429cb. The mechanism specifies the drought as the trigger and details the internal socio-economic cascade, distinguishing it from purely military or pandemic theories. Egypt's survival is explained by its different agricultural base and ideological structure."}
